title: Biology of Aging
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Rafael de Cabo, David G. Le Couteur

items.subtopic: Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY
items.title: Impact of Population Aging on Health Services
items.content: The demographic shift with people over 65 years now exceeding those under 5 highlights the need for healthcare systems to adapt. This change is linked to an exponential increase in chronic diseases among older adults, necessitating a focus on aged care and health service delivery.
keyPoints: People over 65 exceed children under 5
Aging leads to increased incidence of chronic diseases
Health services must adapt to the aging population
items.reference: Biology of Aging · Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY

items.subtopic: Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY
items.title: Theories Explaining Evolutionary Aspects of Aging
items.content: Aging is considered nonadaptive, with theories like mutation accumulation and antagonistic pleiotropy explaining its evolution. These suggest that traits beneficial in early life may become detrimental later, as evolutionary pressures favor early survival and reproduction over longevity.
keyPoints: Aging is nonadaptive
Mutation accumulation theory by John B.S. Haldane
Antagonistic pleiotropy theory by George C. Williams
items.reference: Biology of Aging · Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY

items.subtopic: DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING
items.title: Trade-offs in Aging: Resource Allocation between Reproduction and Soma Maintenance
items.content: The disposable soma theory posits that evolutionary strategies prioritize resource allocation to germ cells over somatic maintenance, leading to age-related damage accumulation. This is influenced by extrinsic mortality rates, with species like humans having longer lifespans due to lower extrinsic mortality.
keyPoints: Trade-off between aging and reproduction
High extrinsic mortality leads to shorter lives and greater reproductive output
Low extrinsic mortality (e.g., in humans) results in longer lives and fewer offspring
Evolution selects for resource utilization favoring germ cells over soma maintenance
items.reference: Biology of Aging · DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING

items.subtopic: NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING
items.title: Understanding Aging: Negligible Senescence and Programmed Aging in Species
items.content: Some species exhibit negligible senescence, where aging does not increase mortality rates, such as Greenland sharks living up to nearly 400 years. Conversely, semelparous animals like Pacific salmon undergo programmed aging and death. The biological hallmarks of aging are interconnected molecular processes that erode health across species.
keyPoints: Negligible senescence: no increase in mortality with age
Examples include clams, sharks (e.g., Greenland shark up to 400 years)
Programmed aging seen in semelparous animals like Pacific salmon
Biological hallmarks of aging are interconnected molecular processes affecting health
items.reference: Biology of Aging · DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING

items.subtopic: GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS
items.title: Genomic Instability: Implications for Aging and Disease
items.content: Genomic instability, characterized by DNA damage from exogenous and endogenous sources, contributes to aging. Mitochondrial DNA is particularly vulnerable due to its proximity to oxidative stress and lack of protective mechanisms. Deficiencies in DNA repair genes can lead to premature aging syndromes such as Werner's syndrome (mutations in WRN gene) and Hutchinson-Guildford progeria syndrome (mutations in LMNA gene).
keyPoints: DNA damage from sources like irradiation, chemicals, oxidative stress.
Mitochondrial DNA is highly susceptible to damage with aging.
Premature aging syndromes linked to deficiencies in nuclear and mitochondrial repair genes.
Examples: Werner's syndrome (WRN gene), Hutchinson-Guildford progeria syndrome (LMNA gene).
items.reference: Biology of Aging · GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS
items.title: Telomere Attrition and Its Role in Aging
items.content: Telomeres, consisting of TTAGGG sequences repeated thousands of times, protect chromosome ends but shorten with cell division. In most cells, this leads to replicative senescence after reaching the Hayflick limit. Telomerase can extend telomeres in certain cells like germ and tumor cells. Shortened telomeres are associated with aging and diseases such as pulmonary fibrosis and aplastic anemia due to telomerase deficiency.
keyPoints: Telomeres consist of TTAGGG sequences repeated thousands of times.
Shortening leads to replicative senescence after the Hayflick limit.
Telomerase can extend telomeres in specific cells.
Shortened telomeres linked to aging and diseases like pulmonary fibrosis, aplastic anemia.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING
items.title: Impaired Autophagy and Protein Aggregation in Aging
items.content: Aging is associated with impaired autophagy-lysosomal and ubiquitin-proteosome systems, leading to the accumulation of damaged proteins and cellular components. This impairment contributes to age-related diseases.
keyPoints: Autophagy declines with aging, affecting proteostasis and organelle turnover.
Accumulation of protein aggregates (e.g., Lewy bodies, neurofibrillary tangles) is linked to impaired autophagy.
Loss of function in autophagy-related genes increases susceptibility to age-related diseases.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING

items.subtopic: LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING
items.title: Caloric Restriction and Lifespan Extension
items.content: Caloric restriction (CR) extends lifespan by activating autophagy and modulating nutrient-sensing pathways. CR-mimetics replicate these effects, influencing key aging pathways.
keyPoints: CR involves reducing food intake to ~30–50% of ad libitum levels, extending lifespan and delaying age-related diseases.
CR activates autophagy and influences pathways like mTOR, IIS, sirtuins, AMPK, and FGF21.
Genetic downregulation or pharmacological inhibition (e.g., rapamycin) of these pathways mimics CR effects.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION
items.title: Mitochondrial Dysfunction and Aging: Mechanisms and Interventions
items.content: Aging is associated with mitochondrial dysfunction characterized by increased electron leakage, decreased ATP production due to impaired complex IV activity, and accumulation of oxidative damage. Nutritional and pharmacologic interventions that delay aging often promote mitochondrial biogenesis through the PGC1α transcription factor.
keyPoints: Mitochondrial DNA damage accumulates with age.
Increased generation of superoxide radicals and hydrogen peroxide occurs due to impaired function.
Oxidative damage affects fat, proteins, and DNA.
Antioxidants have been ineffective in delaying aging.
Interventions that delay aging increase mitochondrial biogenesis via PGC1α.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION
items.title: Cellular Senescence and Its Role in Aging
items.content: Senescent cells, which stop dividing due to telomere shortening or damage mediated by the INK4/ARF system, accumulate with age. They produce inflammatory cytokines like IL-6 and TNF-$\alpha$, contributing to systemic inflammation. Senolytics such as dasatinib, quercetin, and fisetin can eradicate senescent cells and delay age-related pathologies.
keyPoints: Senescent cells stop dividing due to telomere shortening or INK4/ARF system damage.
Accumulation of senescent cells leads to systemic inflammation via SASP (e.g., IL-6, TNF-$\alpha$).
Senolytics like dasatinib, quercetin, and fisetin can delay age-related pathologies by eradicating senescent cells.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.title: Inflammaging: A Key Factor in Aging and Chronic Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: Aging is associated with chronic low-grade inflammation, termed 'inflammaging,' characterized by elevated levels of cytokines such as IL-6 and TNF-$\alpha$, along with increased C-reactive protein and ESR. This inflammatory state contributes to the pathogenesis of various chronic diseases and frailty.
keyPoints: Inflammaging involves low-grade activation of the innate immune system.
Elevated levels of IL-6, TNF-$\alpha$, C-reactive protein, and ESR are common markers.
Factors contributing to inflammaging include SASP, chronic infections like cytomegalovirus, obesity, leaky gut, and NF-$\kappa$B pathway activation.
Inflammation is a key factor in the pathogenesis of many chronic diseases and frailty.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.title: Geroscience: Understanding Aging as a Risk Factor for Chronic Diseases
items.content: Aging is the major risk factor for many chronic diseases, with conditions like dementia, sarcopenia, frailty, and osteoporosis becoming prevalent primarily after age 50. Geroscience explores the relationship between aging biology and disease, highlighting how aging impairs physiological systems, increasing susceptibility to various conditions.
keyPoints: Aging is a major risk factor for chronic diseases such as dementia, sarcopenia, frailty, and osteoporosis.
These age-related disorders are rare below the age of 50 years.
Geroscience studies the relationship between aging biology and disease.
Immunosenescence, endocrine changes, and vascular changes contribute to increased disease susceptibility with aging.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.title: Dysbiosis: Age-Related Changes in the Gut Microbiome and Their Impact on Health
items.content: Aging leads to significant changes in the gut microbiome, characterized by reduced microbial heterogeneity and alterations in specific species and phyla. These dysbiotic changes can impact health, with interventions like fecal microbiome transplantation showing potential effects in animal models.
keyPoints: Aging results in decreased microbial heterogeneity in the gut.
Changes occur in specific species and phyla such as Bacteroidetes and Akkermansia.
Dysbiosis may impact health, with interventions like fecal microbiome transplantation showing potential effects in mice.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: STRATEGIES THAT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING
items.title: Pharmacologic Strategies for Aging Intervention
items.content: Several pharmacologic agents, including resveratrol, rapamycin, spermidine, and metformin, have been identified as potential interventions to delay aging. These compounds target key molecular pathways such as nutrient signaling, mitochondrial proteostasis, and autophagic machinery.
keyPoints: Long-term experimental interventions may include resveratrol, rapamycin, spermidine, and metformin.
Effective aging interventions often converge on molecular pathways like nutrient signaling, mitochondrial proteostasis, and autophagy.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING

items.subtopic: STRATEGIES THAT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING
items.title: Criteria for Validating Antiaging Interventions
items.content: For an intervention to be considered a valid antiaging strategy, it must meet specific criteria: promotion of lifespan and/or health span, validation in at least three model organisms, and confirmation by at least three different laboratories. Examples include CR (caloric restriction), intermittent fasting regimens, and certain pharmacotherapies.
keyPoints: Interventions must promote lifespan/health span, validate across three model organisms, and confirm via three labs.
Examples meeting criteria: CR, intermittent fasting, resveratrol, rapamycin, spermidine, metformin.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.title: CR Delays Aging by Modulating Growth Factors
items.content: Caloric restriction (CR) is defined as a reduction in total caloric intake, typically around 30%, without causing malnutrition. It delays aging by reducing the release of growth factors such as GH, insulin, and IGF-1, which are associated with accelerated aging and increased mortality risk.
keyPoints: CR involves ~30% reduction in caloric intake.
Reduces nutrient-mediated release of growth factors like GH, insulin, and IGF-1.
First discovered by McCay in 1935 before the identification of these hormones.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.title: CR Improves Health Span and Reduces Disease Risk
items.content: In both animal studies and human populations, CR has been shown to increase health span by reducing risks for diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer. The Okinawan population exemplifies this with extended lifespan due to a combination of exercise and low food intake.
keyPoints: CR increases health span by lowering risk for diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer.
Okinawa, Japan: Long-lived population combining exercise with below-average food intake.
Adoption of Western lifestyle in Brazil led to increased weight and reduced life expectancy.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.title: CR's Impact on Human Aging and Health Biomarkers
items.content: The CALERIE trial demonstrated that CR, even at a moderate level of 11.9% reduction over two years, improved quality of life, immune health, cardiometabolic integrity, liver function, and skeletal muscle quality in adults with average weight.
keyPoints: CALERIE trial: 143 adults reduced caloric intake by 25% for 2 years.
Moderate CR achieved: $11.9 ± 0.7	ext{%}$ reduction over two years.
Benefits observed in quality of life, immune health, cardiometabolic integrity, liver function, and skeletal muscle quality.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.title: Benefits of Periodic Fasting on Healthspan and Disease Risk
items.content: Periodic fasting regimens, such as alternate-day fasting or time-restricted feeding, can minimize the risk of developing age-related diseases by promoting metabolic flexibility and reducing inflammation. These strategies may offer health benefits even if overeating occurs during nonfasting periods.
keyPoints: Intermittent fasting regimens have antiaging effects with improved morbidity and longevity observed in studies.

Mice on a high-fat diet with time-restricted feeding showed reduced inflammation, no fatty liver development, and maintained a healthy weight compared to ad libitum fed mice despite similar caloric intake.
Fasting reduces the risk of age-related diseases such as hypertension, neurodegeneration, cancer, and cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.title: Impact of Fasting on Hypertension Management
items.content: Short-term fasting can significantly reduce blood pressure in individuals with borderline or established hypertension. Water-only fasts have shown rapid and effective reductions in blood pressure.
keyPoints: Two weeks of water-only fasting resulted in blood pressure <120/80 mmHg in 82% of subjects with borderline hypertension.
Ten days of fasting cured all hypertensive patients previously on antihypertensive medication.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.title: Fasting and Neurodegenerative Diseases
items.content: Periodic fasting can dampen the consequences of several age-related neurodegenerative diseases in mouse models, though it does not affect amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. It may also enhance the efficacy of chemotherapy against certain tumors.
keyPoints: Fasting cycles are effective against many age-related neurodegenerative diseases in mice, including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Huntington's, and frontotemporal dementia.
Combining fasting with chemotherapy enhances its efficacy against aggressive tumors like glioblastoma or pancreatic tumors, rendering 20–60% of mice cancer-free.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.title: Resveratrol as a CR-Mimetic: Potential Benefits and Limitations
items.content: Resveratrol, found in grapes and red wine, acts as an agonist of SIRT1. It has shown potential to mimic calorie restriction (CR) effects by improving cardiometabolic parameters, reducing inflammation, oxidative damage, tumorigenesis, and enhancing mitochondrial function. While beneficial outcomes have been observed in various animal models, human studies are mixed.
keyPoints: Resveratrol is a polyphenol found in grapes and red wine.
It mimics some metabolic and gene expression changes of calorie restriction (CR).
Beneficial effects include suppression of inflammation, oxidative damage, tumorigenesis, and improvements in mitochondrial function.
Studies show mixed results in humans regarding cardiometabolic benefits.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.title: Rapamycin: Lifespan Extension with Considerations for Adverse Effects
items.content: Rapamycin, an mTOR inhibitor discovered on Easter Island, extends lifespan in various organisms. However, its use in humans is limited by adverse effects such as immunosuppression and hypercholesterolemia. Intermittent treatment may mitigate some risks while extending lifespan.
keyPoints: Rapamycin inhibits mTOR and extends lifespan across multiple species.
Adverse effects include immunosuppression, impaired wound healing, proteinuria, and hypercholesterolemia.
Intermittent rapamycin treatment may increase mouse lifespan with potentially fewer side effects.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.title: Metformin: Beyond Diabetes Management to Potential Anti-Aging Effects
items.content: Metformin, a biguanide used for type 2 diabetes, activates AMPK and inhibits mTOR, leading to lifespan extension in mice. It reduces oxidative damage and inflammation, mimicking CR effects. The TAME trial aims to assess its potential in delaying age-related diseases.
keyPoints: Metformin decreases hepatic gluconeogenesis and increases insulin sensitivity.
It activates AMPK, inhibits mTOR, and reduces mitochondrial complex I activity.
Associated with reduced oxidative damage and inflammation, mimicking CR gene expression changes.
TAME trial (Targeting Aging with Metformin) will enroll 3000 subjects aged 65–79 to assess its effects on age-related diseases.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS
items.title: Exercise as a Key Intervention for Sarcopenia Prevention and Management
items.content: Regular exercise is crucial in preventing or reversing sarcopenia, the age-related muscle wasting. Even moderate levels of physical activity, such as 30 minutes of walking per day, provide significant protective effects, especially in obese individuals.
keyPoints: Exercise can prevent or reverse sarcopenia.
Moderate exercise (e.g., 30-min walking daily) has protective benefits for obese subjects.
Regular physical activity increases independent living duration in older adults.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS

items.subtopic: EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS
items.title: Hormesis: Protective Effects of Low-Dose Stressors on Aging and Lifespan
items.content: The concept of hormesis describes how low doses of stressors or toxins can confer protective effects, enhancing cellular resilience to aging factors like oxidative stress. This adaptive response is observed in various organisms, where exposure to mild stressors leads to increased resistance against more severe conditions.
keyPoints: Hormesis involves beneficial responses to low-dose stressors/toxins.
Adaptive stress responses increase resistance to higher doses of the same triggers.
Examples include yeast cells' anti-stress response and lifespan extension in C. elegans with mild oxidative stress.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS

items.subtopic: CONCLUSIONS
items.title: Understanding Aging Biology for Elderly Care
items.content: Clinicians must grasp the biology of aging to enhance management and care for elderly patients. This understanding is crucial as it informs strategies aimed at delaying aging, reducing age-related disorders, and extending health span.
keyPoints: Importance of understanding aging biology for managing elderly care
Need for strategies that delay aging and reduce age-related disorders
Goal to increase health span for future generations
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: CONCLUSIONS
items.title: Potential of Intermittent Fasting in Human Health
items.content: Intermittent fasting is being explored as a therapeutic intervention for various conditions, including obesity, diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular disease, cancers, and neurologic disorders. While animal studies suggest lifelong health benefits, human trials are currently limited to short-term interventions.
keyPoints: Development of dietary interventions and drugs targeting nutrient-sensing pathways
Human clinical trials exploring intermittent fasting for obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancers, and neurologic disorders
Animal studies indicate lifespan health improvements with intermittent fasting
Current human studies focus on short-term interventions
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY
items.question: Which theory suggests that traits beneficial in early life may become harmful if the organism lives to an older age?
items.explanation: The antagonistic pleiotropy theory suggests that traits beneficial in early life may become harmful if the organism lives to an older age.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY

items.subtopic: Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY
items.question: According to evolutionary theories, why is there minimal selection pressure to maintain health beyond early reproductive years?
options: Due to extrinsic causes of mortality like predation and infection.
Because aging increases susceptibility to disease.
items.explanation: There is minimal selection pressure to maintain health beyond early reproductive years due to extrinsic causes of mortality such as predation, injury, and infection.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY

items.subtopic: Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY
items.question: What is the primary initiating process of aging according to the passage?
options: Stochastic, nonprogrammed changes in nuclear maintenance.
items.explanation: The primary initiating process of aging is most likely to involve stochastic, nonprogrammed changes in nuclear maintenance that influence gene expression and repair.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY

items.subtopic: DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING
items.question: Which theory explicitly hypothesizes that evolution selects strategies prioritizing germ cell maintenance over soma maintenance, leading to age-related accumulation of damage?
items.explanation: The disposable soma theory hypothesizes that finite resources are prioritized for germ cell maintenance over soma maintenance, leading to age-related damage.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING

items.subtopic: DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING
items.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding negligible senescence?
options: Greenland sharks may live up to nearly 400 years.
Negligible senescence means increased mortality with age.
items.explanation: Greenland sharks, which may live up to nearly 400 years, are an example of negligible senescence where there is no increase in mortality rate with age.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING

items.subtopic: DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING
items.question: According to the information theory of aging, which component is relatively stable with aging?
items.explanation: The DNA code is described as a digital information system that remains relatively stable with aging, unlike the epigenome and gene expression.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING

items.subtopic: GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS
items.question: Which of the following is a syndrome associated with mutations in the lamin A gene (LMNA)?
items.explanation: Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome is caused by mutations in the lamin A gene (LMNA).
items.reference: Biology of Aging · GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS
items.question: What role do telomeres play at the ends of linear chromosomes?
options: They counteract the inability of DNA polymerase to replicate chromosome tips.
They increase the rate of cell division.
items.explanation: Telomeres counter the inability of DNA polymerase to replicate the tips of chromosomes.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS
items.question: Which protein family is involved in regulating histones through deacetylation and affects aging and lifespan?
items.explanation: The sirtuins are a family of NAD-dependent proteins that regulate histones through deacetylation and have significant effects on aging and lifespan.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cellular component that can aggregate due to impaired proteostasis with aging?
items.explanation: Leukocytes are not mentioned as aggregating due to impaired proteostasis with aging.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING

items.subtopic: LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING
items.question: What is the effect of caloric restriction on autophagy?
options: It has no effect on autophagy.
It inhibits mTOR without affecting autophagy.
items.explanation: Caloric restriction is known to activate autophagy, which is associated with delayed aging.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING

items.subtopic: LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING
items.question: Which pathway can be manipulated by dietary protein intake to influence lifespan?
items.explanation: The mTOR pathway is activated by amino acids and can be manipulated by dietary protein intake to influence lifespan.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION
items.question: Which of the following is a primary cause of age-related mitochondrial dysfunction?
options: Increased ATP production due to enhanced complex IV activity.
Decreased electron leakage and increased cristae integrity.
Impaired complex IV activity leading to decreased ATP production.
Mitochondrial DNA repair mechanisms becoming more efficient.
items.explanation: Age-related mitochondrial dysfunction is primarily due to impaired complex IV activity, which decreases ATP production.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION
items.question: What contributes to the accumulation of senescent cells in tissues with age?
options: Increased telomere length due to enhanced DNA repair.
Decreased production of inflammatory cytokines like IL-6 and TNF-alpha.
Telomere shortening or damage mediated by the INK4/ARF system.
Enhanced mitochondrial biogenesis via PGC1α transcription factor.
items.explanation: Senescent cells accumulate due to telomere shortening or other damage mediated by the INK4/ARF system.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION
items.question: What is a common outcome of impaired mitochondrial function in aging?
options: Decreased generation of superoxide radicals and hydrogen peroxide.
Reduced oxidative damage to fat, proteins, and DNA.
Increased generation of superoxide radicals and hydrogen peroxide.
Enhanced effectiveness of antioxidants in delaying aging.
items.explanation: Impaired mitochondrial function leads to increased generation of superoxide radicals and hydrogen peroxide, contributing to oxidative damage.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.question: What term describes low-grade activation of the innate immune system associated with aging?
items.explanation: 'Inflammaging' is the term used for low-grade activation of the innate immune system with aging.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.question: Which condition is associated with reduced naïve T cells and impaired memory T cells due to thymic involution?
items.explanation: 'Immunosenescence' is associated with reduced naïve T cells and impaired memory T cells due to thymic involution.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.question: Which of the following is a change in the gut microbiome with old age?
items.explanation: The passage states that there is a decline in microbial heterogeneity with old age.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING
items.question: Which of the following molecular pathways are commonly targeted by effective aging interventions?
options: Nutrient signaling, mitochondrial proteostasis, and autophagic machinery
DNA repair mechanisms and telomere elongation
Oxidative stress reduction and inflammation modulation
Hormonal regulation and immune system enhancement
items.explanation: Effective aging interventions converge on nutrient signaling, mitochondrial proteostasis, and the autophagic machinery.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING

items.subtopic: STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING
items.question: What is a major challenge in assessing the effectiveness of antiaging interventions in mammals?
options: Lack of interest from the scientific community
Short lifespan of animal models leading to artifacts
High cost and complexity of long-term studies
Inability to replicate human aging processes
items.explanation: Experiments lasting the lifetime of animal models are prone to develop artifacts, increasing experimental discrepancies.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING

items.subtopic: STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING
items.question: Which criteria must interventions meet to be considered in this section?
options: Promotion of lifespan and/or health span, validation in at least three model organisms, confirmation by at least one laboratory
Promotion of lifespan only, validation in two model organisms, confirmation by multiple laboratories
Promotion of health span only, validation in four model organisms, no need for confirmation
Promotion of lifespan and/or health span, validation in at least three model organisms, confirmation by at least three different laboratories
items.explanation: Interventions must promote lifespan/health span, be validated in at least three model organisms, and confirmed by at least three labs.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.question: Which nutrient-sensing pathway is NOT mentioned as being involved in mediating the effects of caloric restriction (CR)?
items.explanation: The passage mentions mTOR, AMPK, and insulin/IGF-1 as nutrient-sensing pathways involved in CR but does not mention PI3K/Akt.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.question: What was the primary outcome observed in Okinawa, Japan, that supports the benefits of caloric restriction?
options: Extended lifespan and increased health span
items.explanation: Okinawan people are noted for combining above-average daily exercise with below-average food intake, leading to extended lifespan and increased health span.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.question: What was a significant finding from the Biosphere II project related to caloric restriction?
options: Improvements in insulin, blood sugar, and cholesterol levels
items.explanation: The Biosphere II project showed improvements in insulin, blood sugar, glycated hemoglobin, cholesterol levels, and blood pressure due to severe caloric restriction.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.question: What was the average percentage of caloric reduction achieved by participants in the CALERIE trial over 2 years?
items.explanation: Participants in the CALERIE trial reached a moderate level of caloric restriction, averaging 11.9% ± 0.7% reduction over two years.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.question: Which of the following fasting regimens involves eating only during a specific window each day?
options: 48-h fast once or twice each month
items.explanation: Daily time-restricted feeding involves eating only during a specific window each day.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.question: What is one of the most effective and rapid health benefits observed from fasting?
items.explanation: Fasting results in a reduction in hypertension, with significant improvements noted within two weeks.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.question: Which of the following diseases was not mentioned as being positively affected by fasting in mouse models?
items.explanation: Fasting did not dampen the consequences of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis in mouse models.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.question: What metabolic change occurs during periods of low-calorie intake in periodic fasting?
items.explanation: Periodic fasting leads to an elevation of circulating ketone bodies during low-calorie intake periods.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.question: Which compound is known to mimic some of the metabolic and gene expression changes associated with caloric restriction (CR) by suppressing inflammation, oxidative damage, tumorigenesis, and immunomodulatory activities?
items.explanation: Resveratrol mimics some of the metabolic and gene expression changes of CR by suppressing inflammation, oxidative damage, tumorigenesis, and immunomodulatory activities.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.question: Which compound was originally discovered on Easter Island and is known for extending lifespan in multiple organisms but has potential adverse effects related to immunosuppression?
items.explanation: Rapamycin was discovered on Easter Island and extends lifespan but has adverse effects related to immunosuppression.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.question: Which compound is a physiologic polyamine that induces autophagy-mediated lifespan extension and has been associated with positive effects on cognitive function in older adults?
items.explanation: Spermidine induces autophagy-mediated lifespan extension and has been associated with positive effects on cognitive function in older adults.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.question: Which compound, widely used for treating type 2 diabetes, is being studied in the TAME trial to assess its potential in delaying age-related diseases?
items.explanation: Metformin is being studied in the TAME trial to assess its potential in delaying age-related diseases.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS
items.question: Which of the following is a benefit of regular exercise in older people?
options: Has no effect on bone density.
items.explanation: Exercise is the only intervention that can prevent or even reverse sarcopenia (age-related muscle wasting).
items.reference: Biology of Aging · EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS

items.subtopic: EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS
items.question: What effect does regular exercise have on lifespan according to the passage?
options: Regular exercise does not extend lifespan.
The effect of regular exercise on lifespan is unknown.
items.explanation: While clearly promoting health and quality of life, regular exercise does not extend lifespan.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS

items.subtopic: EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS
items.question: Which combination is more beneficial for muscle mass than either treatment alone?
items.explanation: Alternate-day fasting with exercise is more beneficial for muscle mass than either treatment alone.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS

items.subtopic: CONCLUSIONS
items.question: Which of the following is a primary goal of strategies based on aging biology?
options: Delaying aging and reducing age-related disorders
Increasing the incidence of age-related disorders
Reducing health span for future generations
items.explanation: Strategies based on aging biology aim to delay aging, reduce age-related disorders, and increase health span.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: CONCLUSIONS
items.question: What is being developed and tested in humans according to the passage?
options: Dietary interventions and drugs targeting nutrient-sensing pathways
Strict intermittent fasting regimens for long periods
Short-term clinical benefits from non-dietary approaches
items.explanation: Dietary interventions and drugs that act on nutrient-sensing pathways are being developed and tested in humans.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: CONCLUSIONS
items.question: What is a current focus of most human studies related to intermittent fasting?
options: Long-term health benefits over several years
Short-term interventions over days or months
items.explanation: Most human studies on intermittent fasting are focused on short-term interventions over a few days or months.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY
items.statement: Aging is considered to be an adaptive process shaped by evolution.
items.explanation: Aging is generally considered to be nonadaptive, meaning it has not been shaped by evolution or genetically programmed.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY

items.subtopic: Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY
items.statement: The longevity dividend refers to the concept that interventions slowing aging can delay age-related diseases.
items.explanation: The longevity dividend refers to an intervention that slows the aging process, likely delaying the onset of a wide range of age-related diseases and potentially increasing years of healthy lifespan.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · Biology of Aging | DEFINITIONS OF AGING | MUTATION ACCUMULATION AND ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY

items.subtopic: DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING
items.statement: The grandmother effect explains the development of extended postreproductive survival in humans.
items.explanation: The grandmother effect suggests that evolution favors late-life survival through care provided by long-lived grandmothers, contributing to extended postreproductive survival.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING

items.subtopic: DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING
items.statement: Pacific salmon exhibit negligible senescence.
items.explanation: Pacific salmon undergo programmed aging and death, not negligible senescence.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING

items.subtopic: GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS
items.statement: Telomerase is present in most somatic cells to maintain telomere length.
items.explanation: Telomerase is found in some cells like germ cells and tumor cells, but not in most somatic cells.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS
items.statement: Epigenetic clocks reflect chronological age through consistent changes in DNA methylation patterns.
items.explanation: Epigenetic clocks, such as the Horvath epigenetic clock, are based on consistent age-related changes in DNA methylation.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · GENOMIC INSTABILITY | TELOMERE ATTRITION | EPIGENETIC ALTERATIONS

items.subtopic: LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING
items.statement: Genetic downregulation of the insulin and IGF-1 signaling pathway is associated with decreased lifespan.
items.explanation: Genetic downregulation of this pathway is actually associated with longer lifespans.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING

items.subtopic: LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING
items.statement: Fibroblast growth factor 21 (FGF21) is decreased by protein restriction.
items.explanation: Protein restriction leads to increased production and release of FGF21.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · LOSS OF PROTEOSTASIS | DISABLED MACROAUTOPHAGY | DEREGULATED NUTRIENT SENSING

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION
items.statement: Antioxidants have been effective in significantly delaying aging.
items.explanation: Antioxidants have been investigated but have been ineffective in delaying aging.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION
items.statement: Senolytics can delay the progression of age-related pathologies.
items.explanation: Drugs that eradicate senescent cells, called senolytics, have been shown to delay age-related pathologies.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.statement: Frailty is defined as a multisystem aging syndrome where aging changes are present in most tissues.
items.explanation: The passage defines frailty as a multisystem aging syndrome with aging changes in most tissues.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.statement: Aging is associated with an increase in the production of sex steroids.
items.explanation: The passage states that aging is associated with a reduction, not an increase, in sex steroids.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING
items.statement: The percentage of the population aged ≥65 in the United States is projected to decrease from 13% in 2010 to 19.3% in 2030.
items.explanation: The percentage of the population aged ≥65 is projected to increase, not decrease.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING

items.subtopic: STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING
items.statement: Resveratrol and rapamycin are among the pharmacotherapies proposed for antiaging properties.
items.explanation: Resveratrol and rapamycin are mentioned as pharmacotherapies with potential antiaging effects.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · STRATEGIES THANT INCREASE HEALTH SPAN AND DELAY AGING

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.statement: Caloric restriction has been shown to reduce the risk for diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cancer in monkeys.
items.explanation: Both monkey studies confirmed that caloric restriction increases health span by reducing risks for these diseases.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.statement: The transcription factor Nrf2 is essential for lifespan extension in mice under caloric restriction.
items.explanation: Nrf2 confers most of the anticancer properties but is dispensable for lifespan extension.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.statement: Alternate-day fasting can extend the lifespan of rats by up to 83%.
items.explanation: Rats subjected to alternate-day fasting live up to 83% longer than control animals.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.statement: Fasting has been shown to cure all hypertensive patients without any prior medication.
items.explanation: Ten days of fasting cured hypertensive patients who had previously taken antihypertensive medication.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.statement: Resveratrol has consistently shown lifespan extension benefits across all tested species, including humans.
items.explanation: While resveratrol extends lifespan in many lower-order species and shows some benefits in humans, it does not consistently show lifespan extension benefits across all tested species.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.statement: Spermidine levels decrease during life in virtually all organisms except centenarians.
items.explanation: Endogenous spermidine levels decrease during life in virtually all organisms, with the exception of centenarians.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS
items.statement: Exercise combined with CR has no additive effect on maximal lifespan in rodents.
items.explanation: The combination of exercise with CR has no additive effect on maximal lifespan in rodents.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS

items.subtopic: EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS
items.statement: Hormesis refers to the detrimental effects caused by high doses of stressors or toxins.
items.explanation: Hormesis describes the protective effects conferred by exposure to low doses of stressors or toxins.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · EXERCISE AND PHYSICAL ACTIVITY | HORMESIS

items.subtopic: CONCLUSIONS
items.statement: Intermittent fasting has been shown to improve health throughout the lifespan in most animal studies.
items.explanation: Animal studies indicate that intermittent fasting improves health across the lifespan.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: CONCLUSIONS
items.statement: Most human studies on intermittent fasting have focused on long-term interventions over several years.
items.explanation: Human studies are primarily short-term, focusing on days or months rather than years.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CONCLUSIONS

items.subtopic: DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING
items.assertion: The hallmarks of aging are mechanistic pathways that cause aging.
items.reason: These processes are highly interconnected and impairment in one affects others, influencing health span and lifespan through interventions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why the hallmarks of aging are significant mechanistic pathways.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · DISPOSABLE SOMA THEORY | INFORMATION THEORY OF AGING | NEGLIGIBLE SENESCENCE AND PROGRAMMED AGING | GRANDMOTHER EFFECT | BIOLOGICAL HALLMARKS OF AGING

items.subtopic: MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION
items.assertion: Transplantation of pluripotent stem cells from young donors to old recipients extends lifespan in mice.
items.reason: The recipient's stem cell population can renew itself indefinitely after transplantation.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, the reason given is incorrect; the extension of lifespan is not due to indefinite renewal but likely other factors.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · MITOCHONDRIAL DYSFUNCTION | CELLULAR SENESCENCE | STEM CELL EXHAUSTION

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.assertion: Aging leads to increased susceptibility to cardiovascular disease.
items.reason: Vascular changes including increased arterial stiffness contribute to cardiovascular disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE
items.assertion: Frailty is a condition that only affects skeletal muscle.
items.reason: Frailty involves multiple deficits and impaired function across most tissues.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because frailty affects multiple systems, not just skeletal muscle.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · ALTERED INTRACELLULAR COMMUNICATION | CHRONIC INFLAMMATION | DYSBIOSIS | GEROSCIENCE

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.assertion: Caloric restriction leads to improvements in cardiometabolic integrity.
items.reason: CR induces regulation of core longevity pathways linked to mitochondrial function and stability, lowering inflammation and oxidative stress.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains the assertion as CR affects molecular pathways that improve cardiometabolic health.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: CALORIC RESTRICTION
items.assertion: Caloric restriction was first discovered due to its effects on GH, insulin, and IGF-1.
items.reason: The effects of caloric restriction were first observed by McCay in 1935 before the discovery of these hormones.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because CR was discovered before the understanding of its hormonal effects.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · CALORIC RESTRICTION

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.assertion: Periodic fasting can reduce the risk of developing age-related diseases.
items.reason: Fasting minimizes the risk of hypertension, neurodegeneration, cancer, and cardiovascular disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PERIODIC FASTING
items.assertion: Fasting is associated with increased fertility in humans.
items.reason: Prolonged reduction of calorie intake can decrease fertility.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PERIODIC FASTING

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.assertion: Rapamycin is likely to have limited utility in human lifespan extension due to its adverse effects.
items.reason: Adverse effects related to immunosuppression, impaired wound healing, proteinuria, and hypercholesterolemia limit the potential of rapamycin for human lifespan extension.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why rapamycin's utility in human lifespan extension is limited.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
items.assertion: Metformin increases lifespan by activating autophagy genes such as atg7, atg11, and atg15.
items.reason: The lifespan effects of metformin are mediated through the inhibition of histone acetylases and activation of autophagy genes.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because it's spermidine that increases lifespan by activating autophagy genes, not metformin.
items.reference: Biology of Aging · PHARMACOLOGIC INTERVENTIONS TO DELAY AGING AND INCREASE LIFESPAN
